skill.name: file-path-traversal
skill.description: Security testing skill for identifying and testing path traversal (directory traversal) vulnerabilities in web applications.
skill.summary: Educational security skill for penetration testers to test for local file inclusion (LFI) and path traversal vulnerabilities using industry-standard methodologies.
skill.author: zebbern

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate security testing skill for penetration testers. Static analysis detected 246 patterns, but all are code examples within educational documentation, not executable malicious code. The skill provides testing methodologies for path traversal vulnerabilities (CVE-2021-44228 style testing), including bypass techniques, target file lists, and prevention measures. Content is appropriate for security professionals with proper authorization.

medium_findings.title: Security Testing Content - Requires Authorization
medium_findings.description: This skill contains detailed instructions for exploiting path traversal vulnerabilities. While educational and legitimate for security testing, it should only be used by authorized penetration testers on systems they have permission to test.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The entire document is security testing methodology - legitimate but requires proper authorization to use ethically.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Payload Examples
low_findings.description: Documentation includes common path traversal payloads (../ sequences, URL encoding, null bytes) for testing purposes. These are standard security testing patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are well-known security testing payloads documented for educational purposes.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Reference List
low_findings.description: Lists sensitive system files (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, SSH keys, config files) that attackers might target. This is standard vulnerability assessment knowledge.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard security testing reference material for identifying high-value targets during authorized assessments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Log Poisoning for RCE Escalation
dangerous_patterns.description: Demonstrates log poisoning technique to escalate LFI to remote code execution by injecting PHP code into Apache access logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a real exploitation technique documented for security testing purposes - legitimate in authorized penetration testing context.

dangerous_patterns.title: PHP Wrapper Exploitation
dangerous_patterns.description: Shows how to exploit PHP wrappers (php://filter, php://input, data://, expect://) to read source code or execute commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Real PHP exploitation techniques documented for vulnerability assessment - standard security testing knowledge.

content.user_title: Test web applications for path traversal vulnerabilities
content.value_statement: Web applications often expose sensitive files through path traversal vulnerabilities. This skill provides security professionals with comprehensive testing methodologies to identify and document these security gaps before attackers exploit them.
actual_capabilities: Identify potential path traversal injection points in web application parameters
Generate and test path traversal payloads with various encoding schemes
Document discovered vulnerabilities with proof-of-concept evidence
Provide remediation guidance based on secure coding practices
Test bypass techniques for common input validation filters
Assess impact by identifying accessible sensitive files
limitations: Only tests for path traversal vulnerabilities - does not cover other vulnerability types
Requires user to have proper authorization and legal permission to test target systems
Cannot bypass network-level protections or firewalls automatically
Effectiveness depends on application responses and error message disclosure

use_cases.title: Penetration Testing Engagement
use_cases.description: Security consultants testing web applications for clients need systematic path traversal testing to fulfill OWASP Top 10 coverage requirements and deliver comprehensive vulnerability assessments.

use_cases.title: Security Code Review
use_cases.description: Development teams reviewing legacy code or third-party components can use this skill to identify path traversal vulnerabilities before deployment and implement proper input validation.

use_cases.title: Bug Bounty Hunting
use_cases.description: Independent security researchers participating in bug bounty programs can systematically test for path traversal vulnerabilities across multiple targets with documented methodologies.
use_cases.target_user: Bug bounty hunters and security researchers

prompt_templates.title: Basic Path Traversal Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Test the endpoint [ENDPOINT_URL] with parameter [PARAMETER_NAME] for path traversal vulnerabilities. Start with basic payloads like ../../../etc/passwd and document any responses that differ from normal behavior.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial vulnerability assessment of a file download feature

prompt_templates.title: Encoded Payload Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: The application blocks ../ sequences. Generate URL-encoded and double-encoded path traversal payloads to test filter bypass. Include variations like %2e%2e%2f and ..%252f patterns.
prompt_templates.scenario: Testing against input validation that strips traversal sequences

prompt_templates.title: LFI to RCE Escalation
prompt_templates.prompt: We have confirmed LFI on [ENDPOINT]. Test escalation to remote code execution using log poisoning or PHP wrapper techniques. Document each attempt and response for the vulnerability report.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced exploitation after confirming initial vulnerability

prompt_templates.title: Remediation Report Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a remediation report for discovered path traversal vulnerabilities. Include root cause analysis, secure code examples in [LANGUAGE], and references to OWASP guidelines.
prompt_templates.scenario: Delivering findings to development team for remediation

output_examples.output: VULNERABILITY CONFIRMED: Parameter 'file' is vulnerable to path traversal. Payload '../../../etc/passwd' returned system password file contents (HTTP 200, 1847 bytes). Impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive system files. Recommendation: Implement basename() validation and whitelist allowed filenames.

output_examples.input: Generate remediation guidance for PHP application
output_examples.output: SECURE CODING FIX: Replace direct file inclusion with validated approach. Use basename($_GET['file']) to strip directory paths, validate against whitelist of allowed files, and use realpath() to canonicalize paths before inclusion. See OWASP Path Traversal prevention cheatsheet for complete guidance.

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization before testing any system you do not own
Document all testing activities with timestamps, payloads used, and responses received
Limit testing to minimum necessary to confirm vulnerability - avoid exfiltrating sensitive data
Follow responsible disclosure practices when reporting findings to stakeholders
anti_patterns: Testing production systems without explicit written authorization from the owner
Exfiltrating sensitive data (credentials, PII) beyond what is needed to prove the vulnerability
Using automated tools at high volume that could cause denial of service or data corruption
Sharing vulnerability details publicly before the organization has time to remediate

faq.question: Is this skill legal to use?
faq.answer: This skill is legal when used on systems you own or have explicit written authorization to test. Unauthorized security testing may violate computer crime laws. Always obtain proper authorization before testing.

faq.question: What is the difference between LFI and path traversal?
faq.answer: Local File Inclusion (LFI) is a specific type of path traversal where the application includes local files, often leading to code execution. Path traversal is broader - accessing files outside the intended directory. LFI is typically more severe.

faq.question: Can this skill test for remote file inclusion (RFI)?
faq.answer: This skill focuses on local path traversal. RFI (including remote URLs) is a related but distinct vulnerability. The techniques overlap, but RFI requires different payloads targeting URL inclusion.

faq.question: What should I do if I find a path traversal vulnerability?
faq.answer: Document the vulnerability with proof (sanitized), assess the impact, and report it to the system owner through their responsible disclosure process. Do not exploit beyond confirming the vulnerability exists.

faq.question: Why do some payloads use null bytes (%00)?
faq.answer: Null bytes can truncate strings in older PHP versions (before 5.3.4), bypassing extension checks like .jpg. This is less effective on modern systems but included for completeness in testing legacy applications.

faq.question: How do I know if my testing is causing harm?
faq.answer: Monitor response times and error rates. If you see 500 errors, timeouts, or service degradation, stop testing immediately. Path traversal reads are generally safe, but avoid testing write/delete functionality on production systems.
